params.text:
Personal protective equipment (PPE)

PPE creates a barrier between you and infectious material. Choose it based on the task and the risk, not out of habit. The main items in care are:

  • Disposable gloves - when you may touch blood, body fluids, broken skin or contaminated items. Gloves are single-use for a single task, then removed and binned. They are not a substitute for hand hygiene.
  • Disposable apron - to protect your clothing when there is a risk of splashing or close contact.
  • Fluid-resistant surgical mask - when there is a risk of droplets or splashes to your face, or when guidance requires it.
  • Eye protection - goggles or a visor when body fluids might splash into your eyes.

Putting on (donning) - order

1. Apron → 2. Mask → 3. Eye protection → 4. Gloves. Clean your hands first.

Taking off (doffing) - order

1. Gloves → 2. Apron → 3. Eye protection → 4. Mask. Gloves and apron are usually the most contaminated, so they come off first. Remove your mask last, by the ties or ear loops, without touching the front. Clean your hands immediately after removing all PPE.

Never reuse single-use PPE, and change it if it becomes damaged or heavily soiled. Dispose of used PPE as clinical or infectious waste per your local policy.

params.alt: A care worker wearing gloves, apron and mask

params.question: You have finished personal care and are removing your PPE. Which item should you take off FIRST?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Doffing order is gloves, then apron, then eye protection, then mask - gloves are the most contaminated, so they come off first.
No - the mask comes off last, by its ties or loops, without touching the front. Gloves come off first.
Not quite. The apron is removed second. Gloves come off first because they are usually the most heavily contaminated.
No - eye protection is removed third. Start with gloves, which are the most contaminated item.
